trajectory.description: Observation: Starting with empty workspace. Need to verify that all 10 HumanoidBench tasks are correctly registered, their observation/action space shapes match the description (important for architecture design), and that info dicts contain expected keys. Analysis: Before writing training code, we must confirm env specs to avoid dimension mismatches or Monitor KeyErrors. Decision: Write a quick test script to instantiate each h1hand-*-v0 task, print obs_dim, act_dim, max_steps, reward scale, and info keys after one step. Also write predict.py skeleton based on the task description to have it ready. Run test_env.py as a fast sanity check (should complete in <1 min). No training or eval this iteration.

trajectory.command: for task in h1hand-walk-v0 h1hand-stand-v0 h1hand-run-v0 h1hand-sit_simple-v0 h1hand-balance_simple-v0 h1hand-reach-v0 h1hand-push-v0 h1hand-pole-v0 h1hand-door-v0 h1hand-package-v0; do echo "=== Training $task ===" && python train.py --task $task --total_timesteps 5000 --seed 0; done

trajectory.description: Observation: All 10 tasks verified; info keys match expected. Analysis: Per-task PPO models simpler to implement than multi-task; need training script with VecNormalize and per-task checkpoints. Decision: Write train.py for single-task training with small timesteps, loop over all 10 tasks (5000 steps each) to get baseline, modify predict.py to load per-task model+vecnorm, then submit eval to measure pipeline and baseline success_rate.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 2 train.py crashed with CUDA error 'no kernel image available' on GPU, preventing any training. Analysis: The pre-installed PyTorch CUDA kernels are incompatible with the GPU architecture; network is disabled so we cannot install compatible PyTorch. The only viable path is CPU-only training. This will be slower but should work. Decision: Rewrite train.py to use device='cpu', run one quick training loop over all 10 tasks with small total_timesteps (20000 each, ~2 min per task) to produce valid checkpoints. Set CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES='' in command to disable GPU. Submit eval immediately to test end-to-end pipeline and get a baseline success_rate, which will be low but informative. Time used: ~20 min, well within budget. Later iterations can scale timesteps and improve architecture.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 train.py crashed with 'AttributeError: DummyVecEnv object has no attribute save_running_average' after training walk task. The VecNormalize wrapper likely didn't retain the method properly, or the env variable got reverted. Analysis: We still have a saved walk model (ckpt/h1hand-walk-v0.zip) from the successful model.save call before the crash, but no normalization stats. We can evaluate immediately with a modified predict.py that falls back to random actions for tasks without checkpoints, to get a baseline success_rate and verify the pipeline. Decision: Update predict.py to handle missing checkpoints gracefully, and submit eval now with the existing checkpoint directory. Also write a corrected train.py that manually saves/loads VecNormalize stats using numpy, for future iterations. No training command this iteration to save time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4 eval failed with AttributeError: 'DummyVecEnv' object has no attribute 'save_running_average', caused by predict.py using VecEnv wrappers incorrectly and mishandling reset returns. Analysis: The predict.py used DummyVecEnv/VecNormalize which caused multiple bugs (reset returns tuple, env.close inside episode loop). The SB3 predict method itself doesn't touch env, but our code's obs = env.reset() didn't unpack the tuple leading to downstream errors. A simpler single-gym-env approach per episode, following the minimal template, avoids all VecEnv issues and still works with PPO.load without env. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to use gym.make directly, load model with PPO.load (no env), and manually normalize observations if vecnorm stats exist. Submit eval immediately with existing ckpt/walk model to verify pipeline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Eval failed with exit 1, error trace from predict.py line 56 inside model.predict. Analysis: Possible root causes: (1) model loading/predict fails due to dimension mismatch or environment, (2) the gym warning may be triggering some hard failure, (3) incorrect normalization shape or NaN. We need to isolate whether model.predict works at all with saved checkpoint. Decision: Write debug_predict.py to test loading of walk model and predict with dummy obs. Run it. Also update train.py to properly train all tasks with more steps and save normalization, but for now focus on verifying the pipeline. If debug succeeds, submit eval again immediately to get a baseline (even if low).

trajectory.description: Observation: debug_predict.py confirms model loading and predict works with the existing walk checkpoint. No normalization stats were saved in the tiny training run. Analysis: The current predict.py handles missing checkpoints and missing normalization gracefully, falling back to random actions. An eval submission should now succeed and provide a baseline success_rate (likely 0 given only 2k walk steps). To get a more informative baseline and test training with meaningful steps, we should train a single task (walk) with 100k steps, which should yield some successes. That model + random for others will give ~0.1 success_rate if walk learns. Decision: Write train_one.py to train a single task with configurable timesteps, run it for walk with 100k steps, then submit eval. This measures both training throughput and eval time while establishing a non-zero baseline.

trajectory.description: After 1M steps of walk training (tiny net), success_rate still 0. Walk mean reward only 4.9 — far below 700 bar. Analysis: (1) CPU training with single env (DummyVecEnv) is extremely slow (1831s for 100k steps). With 8.77h left, we must speed up rollouts using parallel SubprocVecEnv to get millions of steps per task. (2) We need to test a scalable training loop: SubprocVecEnv with spawn, 8-16 workers, measure time to 1M steps. (3) We must decide which tasks to prioritize; reach (12000 bar) likely impossible with this compute, so we'll skip it or train it last. Focus on walk, stand, run, sit_simple, pole, door, push, package — those with bars ≤1500 and plausible. (4) Keep network moderate (256,256) and try 2M steps for walk first to see if reward approaches 700, then expand to multiple tasks. Decision: Write train_multi.py using SubprocVecEnv, train walk for 2M steps (16 envs), measure wall time. Use spawn start method. Run it. After training, submit eval to gauge walk performance improvement.
